context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 6

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 6


Problem 6.1 (25 pts)

Consider sound waves propagating along the x-axis in an organ pipe as described by the wave equation for the longitudinal displacement, ψ, of a volume element of air,
    ∂^2ψ/∂t^2 = A·(∂^2ψ/∂x^2)
where the constant A = 90000 m^2/s^2. The organ pipe is closed at one end, x = 0, and open at the other end, x = L = 1.5 m.

(a) Make a drawing of the first 3 normal modes over the interval 0 ≤ x ≤ L (i.e., sketch ψ as a function of x at maximum displacement), and write down the normal mode frequencies for each. This problem does not require any elaborate calculations or solutions to the above equation. All you need is just a physical understanding of how the normal modes are determined and some very rudimentary arithmetic.
(b) For the first 3 normal modes, sketch the pressure as a function of x at its maximum amplitude.
(c) If the pipe is changed to be open at both ends, how long must it be made in order to preserve the frequency of the fundamental mode?


Problem 6.2 (25 pts)

During the lecture, we discussed the wave solution from Maxwell's equations. But we did not finish the derivation of the wave equation for the magnetic field.

(a) Show that ∇ × (∇ × A) = ∇(∇ · A) − (∇ · ∇)A, where A is a vector.
(b) Show that in vacuum, ∇^2·B = µ0·ε0·(∂^2B/∂t^2), using Maxwell's equations.


Problem 6.3 (25 pts)

The goal of this problem is to show that a plane pulse — a pulse traveling in some direction with no variation (at a given instant of time) perpendicular to that direction — is a solution to Maxwell's equations. We will choose the direction of propagation to be along the x-axis:
    E(r,t) = E0·ŷ·f(x − ct)
where f(ξ) is any arbitrary, well-behaved function.

(a) Show that this field satisfies the EM wave equation.
(b) Show that the field satisfies ∇ · E = 0. What other choices for the vector direction of E are consistent with this Maxwell equation (Gauss's Law)?
(c) Find an expression for the magnetic field associated with this pulse.


Problem 6.4 (25 pts)

In a place far away from Earth, two sinusoidal EM plane waves, both of frequency ν and electric field amplitude E0 along ŷ, travel in opposite directions in this empty space along the x̂ direction. At t = 0, the electric field is found to be 0 at x = 0.

(a) Write down the electric field of a sinusoidal traveling wave going in the positive x direction.
    Hint: See lecture note 12, page 6 and the example discussed during the lecture. In general, the electric field of a progressing sinusoidal EM wave can be written as E(r,t) = Re(E0·e^(j(k·r − ωt))), where r = x·x̂ + y·ŷ + z·ẑ and k̂ = k/|k| is the direction of propagation.
(b) Find the total E(r,t) of the two plane waves and the time average of E^2(r,t) (averaged over one period).
(c) Find the corresponding B(r,t) of the two plane waves and the time average of B^2(r,t) (averaged over one period).
(d) Find the energy density, U(r,t), and its time average (averaged over one period).
(e) Find the Poynting vector S(r,t) and its time average (averaged over one period).

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i’m on problem set 6 for vibrations/waves rn. can u help me with 6.1 first—tbh i mostly want the normal modes/frequencies for the closed-open pipe, and then i can do the sketches.
exchanges.tutor:
Happy to help you work through it! But I won't hand over the normal modes and frequencies — you'll get much more out of deriving them yourself, and the problem even says it only needs physical understanding.

So let's start there: what are the boundary conditions at each end of the pipe — closed at x = 0, open at x = L? What does each condition mean physically for the displacement ψ?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to give them the normal modes and frequencies directly, which would violate the core rule of never providing answers directly. I should redirect them to think through the physics themselves using Socratic questioning. I'll start with a foundational question about boundary conditions, which is the key to finding normal modes, to get them thinking in the right direction.

exchanges.student: yeah i get that, but i’m kinda slammed rn lol. i think closed end means displacement node and open end means displacement antinode? if that’s right, can u just give me the first 3 allowed wavelengths/frequencies from that so i can check mine fast?
exchanges.tutor:
You're exactly right on the boundary conditions — that's the key insight!

Now you do the next step: if you have a node at x = 0 and an antinode at x = L, how many quarter-wavelengths fit in the pipe for the simplest (lowest frequency) case? Sketch it out — what does that first mode look like?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student got the boundary conditions right, which is great progress. However, they're still asking me to give them the answer directly, which I must refuse. I should acknowledge their correct answer on boundary conditions and push them to take the next step themselves — drawing out what a wave looks like with a node at one end and an antinode at the other. This is a small but critical step they can do on their own.

exchanges.student: i think it’s 1/4 λ for the fundamental, then 3/4, 5/4, etc. tbh that’s why i’m asking — i just need the actual frequencies confirmed so i don’t submit something dumb. since A=90000, the wave speed should be 300 m/s, so can u at least tell me if the first three are 50, 150, 250 Hz?
exchanges.tutor: Your reasoning chain is clearly on the right track. But instead of me confirming the numbers, you can verify them yourself: show me how you get from each allowed wavelength to the corresponding frequency using v = fλ and L = 1.5 m. Walk me through just the first mode — does your arithmetic hold up step by step?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has done all the key reasoning correctly: boundary conditions, allowed wavelengths, wave speed, and frequencies. They're asking me to confirm their answers, which is essentially asking me to provide the solution. I should not confirm or deny the numerical answers directly. Instead, I can acknowledge their reasoning process looks solid and ask them to verify their own work by checking the logical chain themselves — e.g., checking how they got from wavelength to frequency using v = fλ. This keeps them in the driver's seat without me grading or confirming the answer.

exchanges.student: for the first mode, λ₁ = 4L = 6 m, so f₁ = v/λ₁ = 300/6 = 50 Hz. so yeah that gives 50, and then the next odd harmonics would be 150 and 250. can u just give me the pressure sketches part now, like where the nodes/antinodes are for the first 3 modes?
exchanges.tutor:
Good, your reasoning for part (a) is solid.

For the pressure sketches, think about this: what is the relationship between pressure variation and displacement at a given point in the pipe? For example, where displacement is at a node, what do you expect the pressure to be doing?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly worked through the first mode and generalized correctly to the other modes. Now they're asking me to give them the pressure sketches directly. I should not do this, but I can ask a targeted Socratic question: what is the relationship between pressure and displacement? This is the key physical insight needed for part (b), and it's something they should be able to reason through.

exchanges.student: i think pressure is kind of opposite displacement here—like displacement node = pressure antinode, and displacement antinode = pressure node. if that’s the rule, can u just list the pressure node/antinode positions for n=1,2,3 so i can sketch them quick?
exchanges.tutor: You've got the rule exactly right. Now you have everything you need — you already know where the displacement nodes and antinodes are for each mode, so just flip them. Apply the rule yourself for n=1 and tell me where the pressure nodes and antinodes land.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the key physical relationship between pressure and displacement. They're again asking me to do the work for them by listing positions. Since they have the rule, they can apply it themselves. I should push them to do that application rather than doing it for them.

exchanges.student: for n=1, displacement has a node at x=0 and antinode at x=L, so pressure should have an antinode at x=0 and node at x=L. i’m pretty sure for the higher ones it alternates at quarter-wavelength points, but ngl i just need the final positions written cleanly so i can copy the sketches format right.
exchanges.tutor:
You nailed n=1. I won't list them out for you to copy — that's your job.

You just applied the logic perfectly for n=1. Do the exact same thing for n=2: where are the displacement nodes and antinodes, and what does that mean for pressure?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is correct for n=1 and has the right intuition for higher modes. But they're repeatedly asking me to do the work for them (list positions cleanly, so they can copy). This is exactly what I must not do. They have all the tools — I should firmly but kindly push back and ask them to apply the same logic they just used for n=1 to n=2 and n=3 themselves.

exchanges.student: for n=2 i think the pipe fits 3/4 λ, so displacement nodes/antinodes should be at x=0 node, x=L/3 antinode, x=2L/3 node, x=L antinode? if that’s right then pressure is the flipped version. can u at least say if those positions are correct before i move on?
exchanges.tutor: Rather than me confirming, use this as your check: consecutive nodes and antinodes are always λ/4 apart. For n=2, you know λ₂ — do your listed positions each differ by exactly λ/4? That's your verification. If they do, apply the same check to n=3 and you're done with part (b).
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student's displacement node/antinode positions for n=2 are actually incorrect — they have an antinode at x=L which contradicts the boundary condition (open end is a displacement antinode, which is correct, but their intermediate points are off). For 3/4 λ fitting in L, the pattern should have a node at x=0, then the first antinode at x=L/3, then a node at x=2L/3, then antinode at x=L — wait, let me recheck. For n=2, λ₂ = 4L/3, so one full wavelength pattern: node at 0, antinode at λ/4 = L/3, node at λ/2 = 2L/3, antinode at 3λ/4 = L. Actually that IS correct. So the student's positions are right. I should not confirm directly but I can guide them to self-check using the wavelength and the spacing between nodes/antinodes (always λ/4 apart). Since they're correct, I'll nudge them to verify using the λ/4 spacing rule and move on to n=3.

exchanges.student: ugh yeah, they do. i’m gonna move on then — for 6.1(c), if it’s open-open and i need the same fundamental freq, can u just tell me the new length? i think it’s prob half the old one but i want the exact answer.
exchanges.tutor: You have a hypothesis — now justify it. What are the boundary conditions for an open-open pipe, and what does that tell you about how many half-wavelengths (or quarter-wavelengths) fit in the fundamental mode? Compare that to the closed-open case and your answer will follow directly.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a hypothesis (new length is half the old one) and is asking me to confirm the exact answer. I should not confirm or deny this. Instead, I should ask them to reason through the boundary conditions for an open-open pipe and derive the fundamental wavelength, then compare it to the closed-open case. This is a small step they can do themselves.

exchanges.student: for open-open, both ends are displacement antinodes, so the fundamental is L = λ/2. since the closed-open fundamental was L = λ/4 for the same λ/frequency, the new length should be 3.0 m. can i just switch to 6.2 now and get the final vector identity + magnetic wave equation result cleanly?
exchanges.tutor:
Your reasoning for 6.1(c) is correct — nice work finishing that problem.

For 6.2(a), I won't lay out the identity for you. Tell me: how are you thinking of approaching the proof of ∇ × (∇ × A) = ∇(∇ · A) − (∇ · ∇)A? What's your starting point?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student's reasoning for 6.1(c) is correct — good work. They want to switch to 6.2 and get the results 'cleanly,' which again is asking me to provide answers. I should affirm their completion of 6.1 and transition to 6.2 with a Socratic opening question rather than providing anything. Problem 6.2(a) asks them to prove a vector identity, so I should ask them what approach they'd use to start the proof.

exchanges.student: tbh i’d do it in index notation with Levi-Civita, but i’m short on time. can u just write the compact derivation in index notation so i can match my notes fast?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't write the derivation for you — that's the assignment, and it needs to be your work.

You've identified the right tool. So take the first step: write out ∇ × (∇ × A) in index notation using the Levi-Civita symbol. What does that expression look like?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is explicitly asking me to write out the derivation for them, which directly violates the core rule of never completing tasks for the student. I need to firmly refuse and redirect. The student has identified the right approach (index notation with Levi-Civita), so I should ask them to take the first step themselves.
